data.title: Fresh Market Highs, Here's What I See
data.description:
Introduction

The headlines ran bullish as we crossed the 46,000 level on the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI). It's not just the Dow; the S&P 500 Index (SP500), NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND), and market darling group, the Magnificent Seven, are all also at all-time highs after rebounding from April lows and continuing the "most hated V-shaped rally (0:15)," which appears to be all gas, no brakes, as the kids say.

It's times like these I feel like grounding is important for investors, as irrational exuberance plays out around us.

Look at just how far the Dow has come in the last century (95 years, I can only go back to 1930). This really puts into perspective the explosive growth of the market post-GFC. The growth has been so steep in the last 15 years.

It's easy looking at that chart to say that the answer is to always hold on tight. It would be a cheap shot to show a picture of the NIKKEI 225 Index (NKY:IND) from 1989-2023, where they had lost decades, plural. Current America is not 1990s Japan, and I won't pretend that it's anywhere close.

However, there are a few major risks to consider for investors in these broad market indexes that I think we should consider here.

Valuation Risk

The implication of this market being at all-time highs is also that valuations are at all-time highs. Beware that in past market conditions when valuations were this high, the following years usually had lower returns than expected and definitely lower than the previous years that led up to the valuation peak.

Sometimes, crashes follow. Other times, it's just long periods of low market performance as earnings catch up to valuations. Those periods seem less common and shorter in our modern era.

I'm not prescribing a crash or some major correction here, for the record. I'm bullish on the market, still sticking to my call back in June; I think we could go another 10% higher on the Nasdaq by the end of the year, which would bring the S&P and Dow along with it.

Knowing that we are in a bubble is different from knowing when it will pop.

All that is to say that we should be expecting the unexpected from here and that being prudent matters most in times like this when valuations are frothy. When valuations crater, it's usually not due to a surge in earnings justifying them. It's usually due to a cratering share price.

Even though we are at all-time highs in the market and at relative highs in valuation, we are still below the earnings per share highs we saw in 2022. This is not as big a deal as it may seem but it is worth noting as we enter an economy that is growing more from AI CapEx than consumption.

Median earnings for U.S. large caps are still trending in the right direction, part of why I am still bullish, but this chart is telling of the market conditions in a lot of ways.

Concentration Risk

I've hammered this point before, but it bears mentioning again: the S&P 500 (SPX) and other major indexes like the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) have a concentration problem where they are incredibly top-heavy. The top ten stocks make up over 50% of the Nasdaq 100, with its top stock, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), taking up nearly 10%.

The Dow Jones has a different problem, because while it is more naturally concentrated due to its holding of 30 stocks, it uses a price-weighting scheme that makes it top-heavy as well, but in a different way. Its largest holding is The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) at over 10%.

To be fair to the Magnificent Seven, stocks that are the cause of a lot of this movement, you can see their clear correlation with all of these market indexes, even the Dow. You can sort the indexes by concentration of mega-cap tech, high to low (in our case, top to bottom of this next chart), and see the same performance patterns in all of them, but less and less pronounced—read as "volatile."

When you sort the S&P 500 by holdings, going from most to least concentrated, you get the same effect. This timeline is shorter due to the limited lifespan of the top 20 ETFs, TOPT.

Economic & Fed Risk

Warning: I'm going to be a bit of a wonk and link a lot of data here so I can keep this short but deep and allow folks to go deeper if they want.

On the note of economic risk, I think we are in overall decent economic shape given we see central bank rates come down as they are expected to over the next few months, and over a longer timeframe, inflation is still running a little hot and is getting hotter, but unemployment is still below its historical average, despite weakening labor data and heavy revisions.

This confluence has put the Federal Reserve in a monetary policy "rock and hard place" with its Federal Funds Rate, which drives prevailing interest rates, and it has been looking like they will begin another cutting cycle and, over the long term, will keep up quantitative easing practices as a "default" policy mode even if it means accepting higher inflation levels.

All the while, the U.S. Treasury is looking to expand the national debt by another $1.6T in H2 2025, continually pushing more money into the system as they borrow to spend while cutting revenue from the largest sources (income and corporate taxes).

At the simplest level where it is still useful, monetary and fiscal policy are aligning in such a way that we should expect an increase in overall liquidity, which is beneficial for asset prices.

We commonly measure liquidity with money supply, or M2.

A derailing of this narrative, say if we have a Fed that begins to call for rate hikes, would be a risk that investors need to watch out for. The market is currently pricing in a very dovish Fed, and if they don't get it, they may cause a scene. A hawkish Fed could cause further contraction of M2, which would in turn pull asset prices with it.

What I'm Doing About It

The answer to these concerns is the same: manage your risk. That looks different for different investors — one of the beauties of DIY investing.

While some investors use options; others prefer to manage risk via position sizing, which is my favorite to advocate and use, personally and professionally.

Make sure not to use "position sizing" as a way to push yourself entirely out of the market. Even at all-time highs, there is a chance we go higher. Regardless of all the warnings I can give, prices can go up for any reason, and they often do in the short term. The data I'm pointing to here is all long-term, and so it's important to stay invested for the long term, even if your positions are kept small to ensure that your risk tolerance is met.

I am considering trimming back my equity positions from where they are now, nearly fully deployed, but my optimistic view of the market's technicals has me staying in for now. I am positioned well, however, and have not overexposed myself to the market if there is a deep pullback or a correction to valuations.

However, as I have been writing about, I believe it is in investors' best interests to consider markets outside U.S. large caps, which are not at all-time highs and could have a significant comeback. If anything, the expected returns are higher due to buying in at lower valuations. While cheap can stay cheap forever, and there are great arguments for American exceptionalism, the charts don't lie. Sometimes, betting on small caps and ex-U.S. stocks is the way to go.

I think we may be entering a phase like that over the next few years. That will be a longer-term trend and may take even longer to play out, depending on how long the AI party continues in full swing.

That party is still on in the U.S. for now, and I will not be reducing my U.S. exposure, but I am adding to other positions currently, and I think it's very prudent to consider the stocks left behind as we reach these fresh all-time highs.

Conclusion

With the major indexes hitting new highs, I believe it's a good moment for investors to ground themselves and be reminded of the major risks still at play in this market, like valuations running away from earnings (ex-Mag 7), index concentration in the top firms, and looming economic risks dependent on monetary and fiscal policy decisions out of the hands of investors.

The best way to deal with it is twofold:

  1. Reevaluate your risk tolerance, making sure that you are positioned so that a deep, prolonged correction wouldn't force you to sell, either from a solvency or psychological standpoint.
  2. Consider opportunities to diversify when U.S. markets are at all-time highs, especially for investors with portfolios heavy on U.S. large-cap stocks.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Nvidia: Undervalued At Current Levels, Poised For Multi-Year Growth
data.description:
Some investors may look at the title of this article and think that after being up 31.95% YTD and 51.48% over the past year, there is no way shares of NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) can break out again. The bear case has revolved around the amount of capital being allocated toward CapEx eventually slowing, causing sales in GPUs to plateau or decline. Eventually I do believe this may occur, and while my initial bull case for NVDA had an expanding CapEx allocation period of 18-24 months, I think that there is still a multi-year buildout ahead of us. I read through all the main technology earnings reports, and I do not see any indication that CapEx is slowing or proprietary chips will impact the demand for NVDA’s GPUs. Outside of the hyperscalers Oracle Corporation (ORCL) and CoreWeave (CRWV) released critical information that supports an extended CapEx buildout that includes NVDA’s GPUs. On ORCL’s recent earnings call, Safra Catz (ORCL CEO) indicated that their remaining performance obligations reached $455 billion which was an increase of 359% YoY and they are expecting to allocate $35 billion toward CapEx this fiscal year. CoreWeave reported that its revenue backlog increased by 85% to $30.1 billion in Q2 2025. I believe that NVDA delivered one of the best earnings reports I have ever read, and while its shares have generated enormous returns for long-term shareholders, I believe they still have the ability to breakout well past $200.

Following up on my previous article about NVIDIA Corporation

At the beginning of June, I wrote an article about NVDA (can be read here) where I discussed how its explosive growth could exceed the market's expectations. Since then shares of NVDA have appreciated by 24.32% while the S&P 500 has gained 10.03%. I felt that NVDA’s free cash flow (FCF) growth and massive share buybacks were being looked over by the market as their ash generation and strategic initiatives positioned NVDA to outperform. NVDA’s expansion into sovereign AI, enterprise AI, and robotics, plus the Blackwell platform ramp, significantly expanded its total addressable market, which was bullish for its top-line revenue growth. NVDA just delivered a blowout quarter with some of the best results I have ever seen from a company. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am more bullish than ever for NVDA and believe shares will breakout well past the $200 level.

NVIDIA Corporation delivered a blowout quarter and the results are very bullish

I have never seen the amount of growth NVDA has accomplished in such a short period of time. NVDA went from generating $26.91 billion in revenue during 2021 and $26.97 billion during 2022 to growing their revenue by 125.85% ($33.95 billion) in 2023 to $60.92 billion and then by another 114.20% ($69.58 billion) in 2024 to $130.5 billion. After the first 2 quarters in 2025, NVDA has already increased its trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue by 26.61% ($34.72 billion) to $165.22 billion, and there is a good chance that they finish the year having generated around $200 billion, which would be an increase of 53.26% ($69.5 billion) YoY. For me, the story isn’t just about the revenue growth as NVDA’s profitability is growing by massive amounts and their margins are off the charts for a semiconductor company. Last year NVDA generated $97.86 billion in gross profit for a margin of 74.99%. NVDA’s operating income came in at $81.45 billion for a margin of 62.42%, and its net income was $72.88 billion, which was a 55.85% profit margin. In the TTM, NVDA has generated $115.4 billion in gross profit for a margin of 69.85%. Its operating income is $95.98 billion, and its net income is $86.6 billion, which are margins of 58.09% and 52.41%. The fact that NVDA has a profit margin that exceeds 50% with this type of growth at this scale is incredible, and no other company in the Magnificent Seven can match this.

When I look at NVDA on a quarterly basis, the numbers from Q2 are incredible. NVDA generated $46.74 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 55.6% ($16.70 billion) YoY and 6.08% ($2.68 billion) QoQ. NVDA’s gross profit increased 49.96% ($11.28 billion) YoY and 26.94% ($7.19 billion) QoQ to $33.85 billion. In Q2, NVDA’s gross profit margin was 72.42%, which paved the way for some of its highest operating and net income margins. NVDA was able to grow its operating income by 52.56% ($9.8 billion) YoY and 31.44% ($6.8 billion) QoQ to $28.44 billion. NVDA’s net income grew 59.18% ($9.82 billion) YoY and 40.73% ($7.65 billion) QoQ to $26.42 billion. NVDA’s operating and profit margins were 60.84% and 56.53% in Q2. The fact that NVDA operated at a margin above 60% and netted over 55% on the bottom line is unlike anything I have seen from a company that brought in over $45 billion in revenue over a 3-month period. This type of growth is making their forward estimates look inexpensive.

When I look at the EPS projections for NVDA and the rest of the Magnificent Seven from the analyst community, shares of NVDA look very undervalued. I also added Costco Wholesale (COST) in my valuation table to emphasize how undervalued NVDA is. NVDA is expected to generate $4.49 of EPS in 2025 and trades at 39.46 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2-years the analyst community expects EPS to grow its EPS by 67.26% ($3.02) to $7.51. NVDA is trading at 23.11 times 2027 earnings with almost 70% growth expected over the next 2 fiscal years, while Apple (AAPL) is trading at 26.20 times 2027 earnings with less than 20% growth on the horizon. COST on the other hand generated $268.78 billion in revenue during the TTM and has a profit margin of 2.92%, as their net income was $7.84 billion, yet it trades at 53.16 times 2025 earnings. COST is expected to generate 22.49% of EPS growth over the next 2-years and trades at 43.40 times 2027 earnings. When I look at the amount of growth that NVDA is projected to generate, its current profitability and margins, the current valuation looks very inexpensive, especially in an environment where the CapEx spend isn’t deteriorating.

The combination of AMZN, MSFT, META, and GOOGL allocated $88.25 billion toward CapEx in Q2 2025. This was an increase of 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. These companies have allocated $291.35 billion toward CapEx in the TTM and their not stopping any time soon. When I look at the quarterly consolidated totals, the growth rate is actually increasing. In Q2 2024 the combination of these 4 companies increased their CapEx spend QoQ by 19.33% ($8.56 billion) to $52.85 billion. In Q2 2025, the QoQ increase from them was 22.73% ($16.34 billion) to $88.25 billion. These companies are spending more on CapEx and now companies like ORCL are trying to buy up as many GPUs as they can. I am not seeing any indication that allocations toward CapEx is slowing down and when I read all the earnings reports each of these companies is committing more capital toward CapEx. This makes me believe that the analyst community may be conservative in their estimates and shares of NVDA could be very undervalued, as they could outperform the massive growth that is already expected.

NVIDIA Corporation is positioned for growth and I think shares are undervalued

NVDA’s Q2 results showcased their exceptional operational performance and strategic advancements across its business segments. Data Centers remained NVDA’s growth engine as it generated $41.1 billion in revenue which was an increase of 56% YoY and 5% QoQ. The Blackwell Data Center revenue increased 17% QoQ as the production of Blackwell Ultra recognized high demand. NVDA’s GB300 platform began production shipments in Q2 as its factory conversions supported its ramp to approximately 1,000 racks per week. NVDA is expected to expand the amount of racks per week it generates in Q3. NVDA also announced that its RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPU is being adopted by global enterprises, which include Disney (DIS), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). NVDA collaborated with France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and the U.K. to build Blackwell AI infrastructure, which included the world’s first industrial A.I. cloud for European manufacturers. I had indicated in my previous article that Sovereign AI would be important for NVDA, and we are seeing significant progress in this area. NVDA was able to help optimize sovereign large language models with NVIDIA Nemotron that were available on Perplexity.

One of the most important things to come out of Q2 was that NVDA is forecasting that there will be a $3-$4 trillion A.I. infrastructure market by the end of the decade which is based on the rapidly escalating annual A.I. investments. NVDA believes that the combination of AMZN, META, MSFT, and GOOGL will spend $600 billion annually in the next 2-years. I think that NVDA is positioned for sustained growth that will last at least 3-5 years, maybe longer. NVDA has become an A.I. infrastructure company that provides full-stack systems which integrates chips, networking, systems, software, and algorithms. I believe that this is what the skeptics are overlooking as NVDA has expanded its operations beyond chips to generate recurring revenue streams. We are also still in the early stages of full self-driving, humanoid robotics, and automated factories/warehouses which will all have continuous innovation and increased demand. I think that as the next several years unfold a larger emphasis will be put on their software leadership as it will generate more revenue through platforms like CUDA, TensorRT-LLM, and Dynamo as they are already being used by more than 6 million developers. NVDA has a large opportunity to generate recurring revenue as they become more entrenched throughout the A.I. ecosystem.

Risks to investing in NVIDIA Corporation

While I am very bullish on NVDA there are risk factors that investors should consider before allocating capital toward it. A large portion of their revenue is comprised of 4 major companies which include Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT). If these companies have breakthroughs in their proprietary chips than can offset the amount of GPU’s needed from NVDA it could allow them to allocate less capital toward CapEx. If this happens NVDA’s revenue and profitability could come under pressure as their forward growth is tied to GPU sales. The other large risk is that software gets so advanced that companies won’t need as many GPUs to train their models which could also hurt NVDAs top and bottom line. At some point the demand for NVDA’s chips will become cyclical the only question is when does that happen. Investors are currently frontrunning when the shift will happen and there could be less time than I believe as to when this occurs. Investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward NVDA.

Conclusion

I still believe NVDA is one of the largest opportunities in the market despite being a $4.3 trillion company. The reality is that over the TTM NVDA has generated $86.6 billion in net income and is trending to produce over $100 billion in profitability if their margins and growth remains sustainable. NVDA just approved an additional $60 billion of buybacks on top of the $14.7 billion they had left on the previous authorization. As NVDA becomes more profitable they will likely reward shareholders through larger buybacks which will help them scale EPS. Based on what I see happening from the hyperscalers and other technology companies in their CapEx allocations I think that NVDA has fortified their operations with a multi-year roadmap that keeps their customers trapped within their ecosystem. None of the hyperscalers can take their foot off the gas when it comes to their dependence on NVDA’s chips and services otherwise they run the risk of falling behind their competitors if their wrong. NVDA is expected to generate around $54 billion of revenue with a GAAP gross profit margin of 73.3% compared to 72.42% in Q2. This means that NVDA could exceed $30 billion of profitability in Q3 and I am expecting that if this happens the analyst community will raise their forward EPS estimates and shares of NVDA will breakout to the upside well past $200.

data.title: Core & Main: The Growth Narrative Is Under Huge Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

I downgraded to a hold rating for Core & Main, Inc. (NYSE:CNM), as I did not like how the macroeconomy was moving; coupled with the weak guidance, I believed the stock would not see any positive traction. While the share price has risen since March, I believe my rating was right, as the stock plummeted right after the 2Q25 earnings and is now back to near March's share price level. This 2Q25 quarter showed it had reinforced the bear case. With residential demand collapsing and internal execution missteps, I believe CNM's growth story is breaking down. I downgrade to a sell rating.

Poor 2Q25 Results

Unfortunately, CNM's 2Q25 results have made me even more bearish. Net sales saw $2.093 billion, which was a 6.6% y/y growth on a reported basis, but organic growth was only ~5%. Growth was also not broad-based. While pipes and storm drainage grew, meter product sales declined y/y. End-market performance told a similar story. Municipal demand remained stable, and non-residential held up, but weakness in residential lot development overshadowed these positives. Given that lot development is a forward indicator of future activity, this trend is very concerning. Even more troubling, profitability deteriorated despite gross margin improvement. Gross margin expanded 40 bps to 26.8%, but this was entirely erased by a lack of cost discipline. SG&A grew 12.7% y/y to $302 million, nearly double the rate of sales growth, pushing SG&A to 14.4% of sales versus 13.6% in 2Q24. This drove adj. EBITDA margin down 40 bps to 12.7%, with adj. EBITDA up only 3.5% y/y.

The Bear Thesis

I believe the bear case for CNM is playing out in real time. At a high level, CNM appears to be in the middle of a perfect storm, where an important end market is collapsing at the very same moment internal costs are getting out of control. This is going to hit both the top and bottom lines simultaneously, and I don't expect the market to take this positively.

The most urgent issue is the continued softness in residential lot development, which represents ~20% of CNM's sales. In just one quarter, management's outlook has changed violently from "Flat" to "Down Low Double Digits" for FY25. This is a massive downgrade, and I don't think this represents just a routine adjustment. Management's commentary only made this concern sound even worse. Specifically, they noted projects in key markets are not just being scaled back but, in many cases, stopped entirely. Viewed from another angle, there is a huge hole in CNM's revenue base (20%) that is going to heavily weigh on performance.

Like I have noted above, there was a clear lack of cost discipline, and I think the really negative takeaway here is that this elevated cost structure is likely to stay for the near term. Management attributed the increase to everything from rising employee benefit costs to integration challenges from acquisitions, but whatever the drivers are, the reality is that this cost surge is going to eat into profitability. I don't think the announced "cost-out actions" will do anything to instill confidence. Management itself admitted the bulk of savings won't be realized until FY26, which basically means 2H25 will face the same pressure.

Aside from these, CNM is also showing terrible execution missteps that add to the bear case, most notably in meter products, where sales fell 7% y/y due to project delays. This misstep is damaging because it shows CNM problems are not just due to the macro-sensitive residential market but also within its own operational execution. Management pointed to a difficult 2Q24 comparison (saw 48% growth in the category), but it doesn't change the fact that CNM failed to manage customer timelines or deliver on schedule.

Put together, the end result is that management was forced to cut FY25 adj. EBITDA guidance, confirming that margins will contract in FY25. In my view, this is more than just a missed quarter; I think it has put a huge dent in the prior growth narrative and is a serious hit to management credibility.

Valuation

CNM valuation has seen a sharp reset from low-20x pre-results back to its 10-year average of 18x forward PE. I think there are more downsides to come from here. Consensus is still expecting EPS growth in the high teens for FY26 and low teens in FY27. I truly wonder if that will come true if 20% of sales are effectively coming to a halt and costs are growing. If what management said is right, and the US housing market continues to be in a slump, I see a good chance for CNM to miss its own guidance and consensus estimates. This should trigger waves of estimate downgrades, further driving multiples downwards.

Risk

If CNM is able to consistently expand gross margins through pricing and sourcing initiatives, it may offset the SG&A pressure more effectively than I expected. Furthermore, the resilient municipal market, supported by infrastructure spending and a stable non-residential sector, could prove strong enough to better cushion the sharp decline in residential demand.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for CNM. The collapse in residential lot development, increasing SG&A burden, and poor execution are negative for both growth and profitability. Management's cut to FY25 EBITDA guidance highlights that this is more than just a soft quarter but a reset of expectations. With consensus estimates still overly optimistic and valuation likely to compress further, I see more downside risk ahead.

data.title: My Doctor Pays Me 12.7%: THQ
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Rarely are objects as simple as they appear on the outside. A simple wristwatch is comprised of many different capacitors and wires. Even in older-style wristwatches, gears and cogs play a crucial role in making the watch function. Oftentimes, hammers are significantly more complicated than just a hammerhead on top of a piece of wood. There are various pieces securing the hammerhead, and multiple steps to create a strongly forged hammerhead and a well-prepared handle.

This oversimplification expectation can lead many to frustration when it comes to investing in the market, especially when we'd like to make oversimplified assessments of different sectors.

A few weeks ago, we had an elaborate discussion on the pharma industry and how it isn’t a monolith of direct competitors. This means that companies maintain exclusives for specific ailments, which are patent-protected for decades. On the cheaper side of the fence, we have generics and biosimilars. However, these are rarely manufactured in the U.S., and this makes tariffs a universal problem across the industry. It also cannot be stressed enough that the U.S. healthcare system is a complex web of insurers, intermediaries, providers, distributors, and regulators; so higher patient costs cannot be pinned solely on biopharma companies.

Another Stellar Healthcare Fund

Today, we will discuss abrdn Healthcare Opportunities Fund (NYSE:THQ), yielding 12.7%, an actively managed CEF with 112 holdings, with a higher focus on healthcare equipment, which includes medical devices, diagnostic and laboratory equipment, hospital equipment, consumables, etc.

During Q2, the top holdings in THQ's portfolio have delivered strong earnings growth, and some have even raised their guidance and outlook. Industry leaders like Abbott Laboratories and Thermo Fisher have made commitments to invest billions to expand their U.S. manufacturing presence.

"Made in USA" - in practice, this concept is more complex than it appears. Let us review the example of Boeing (BA), which assembles its aircraft in America. A built aircraft is composed of thousands of components sourced from dozens of countries, as can be seen on this page. The final product represents a complex and integrated global supply chain.

Like automotives and aircraft, medical devices are also built with components sourced globally. Studies estimated ~70% of medical devices sold in the U.S. could be impacted by tariffs. Even if they have a "Made in USA" tag, tariffs on components, raw materials (like specialized metals or plastics), or sub-assemblies can significantly increase the cost of production, regardless of where the final assembly takes place.

Medtronic's CEO recently estimated that new tariffs could add up to $950 million annually to the company's gross cost of goods.

"Sixty percent of our revenue globally is tied to manufacturing in the U.S. We have 35 manufacturing and distribution centers in the U.S. We invest $5 billion a year in these factories." - Geoff Martha, CEO of Medtronic

Unless the situation gains more clarity or secures necessary exclusions from sweeping tariffs on imports, we believe that the hospital, the healthcare system, and the patient are the ones to bear the costs.

So, let’s shift our focus to THQ. One of the benefits of investing in sector-focused CEFs that pay large distributions is the ability to maintain our income despite inevitable stock price movements.

Since its inception in 2014, the CEF has maintained steady monthly distributions, with a significant raise in early 2024 following the acquisition of Tekla funds by Abrdn. THQ’s $0.18/share monthly distribution calculates to a 12.7% annualized yield.

Looking at the price movements of THQ, there have been numerous times in its 11-year operating history where people could have simply deemed it a bad investment. But steady monthly distributions supported excellent opportunities to compound returns.

Investors must understand that there are multiple concepts involved – there is market price, then there's NAV, there's distribution income, and there's total return. Market price and total returns are only useful if someone decides to sell their investment for good. Income is ready to use as the investor sees fit. NAV pattern shows whether the distributions are constructive or destructive. Income investors must understand these concepts to set their investment objectives right.

As part of its active investment strategy, THQ operates with 23% leverage, which carries an interest rate of SOFR + 0.95%. The CEF reported $118.9 million in net unrealized gains (on a tax basis) sitting in the portfolio at the end of March 2025. This compares to $44.6 million in distributions paid to shareholders during the first six months of the fund’s fiscal year. While components of THQ's distribution from time to time may be classified as return of capital, it would be economically incorrect to call it a return of principal.

The transformative power of AI is undeniable, but it will not replace our fundamental need for a check-up, a necessary treatment, or the medication that saves lives. Amidst today's AI-driven valuations and the backdrop of trade tariffs, the healthcare sector's current discount presents a compelling opportunity to invest in a critical industry with an excellent margin of safety, positioning investors for strong total returns as its long-term necessity becomes clearer.

Conclusion

When it comes to investing in sectors that are potentially exceptionally complicated, like the healthcare sector, where there are multiple moving parts, whether you're investing in bioscience, pharmaceuticals, research, or vaccines, the list goes on, it can be beneficial to leverage the experience of highly qualified portfolio managers to navigate the depth of a sector that could take someone a lifetime to understand fully.

When I'm sitting in the role of a portfolio manager wanting to oversee a wonderful retirement portfolio that provides me with massive sums of recurring income to live off of today and enjoy tomorrow, I don't always have the time to become an expert on every pharmaceutical company or healthcare opportunity. As a portfolio manager, I will, much like a general contractor, hire experts to do the work for me. I'll oversee the experts and ensure that their services meet my expectations, but I also recognize that I can leverage their expertise to my benefit. THQ is a well-run fund with a stellar track record, providing me with exactly what I'm looking for: Strong total returns over the long run and wonderful income in the immediate.

When it comes to your retirement, I don't expect you to be an expert in every single investment opportunity either. I spend thousands of hours annually researching and investing in different companies, eliminating tens of thousands of ideas to whittle them down to more than 100 that are within my Model Portfolio. I also recognize that I have limitations on my abilities as well as the abilities of my team. This is why leveraging funds like THQ benefits both you and me as portfolio managers. Knowing when to hire and when to flex our abilities to maintain control over specific areas is essential.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Centene: Temporary Headwinds, Guidance Steady
data.description:
Investment thesis

At first glance, Centene (NYSE:CNC) doesn’t seem like an obvious investment opportunity: due to an unexpected quarterly loss and strong industry headwinds, its share price dropped by a staggering 54%. But scratch beneath the surface and you get a different picture. A solid balance sheet, good profitability, and a growing managed care market instantly make the company look more interesting. Add in that said headwinds seem very much temporary rather than structural and that the management team is making concrete efforts to restore margins, and we have, in my opinion, a company that appears to be deeply undervalued. I therefore recommend a "strong buy."

Company information

Centene Corporation is a healthcare company that provides programs and services to underinsured and uninsured families, and commercial organizations in the U.S. According to the 10-K report, the company conducts its business via four segments. CNC's fiscal year ends on December 31.

The company is one of the largest players in the industry, with a TTM revenue of around $160 billion. Despite the temporary weakness [which we will discuss in detail later] in recent financial performance, CNC's average growth rates across all key P&L lines over the last decade are impressive.

In addition to impressive growth across key metrics over the last decade, CNC also boasts an "A-" profitability grade from Seeking Alpha Quant.

Temporary headwinds

I see three key reasons behind the sharp decline in Centene’s share price over the last year.

First up is the company’s surprise loss in Q2 of this year, triggered by a downward adjustment in ACA risk transfer revenues. Following a new actuarial analysis, the company had to lower its expected annual revenue by $1.8–$2.4 billion. There’s no doubt that this adjustment hit the top line hard and brewed uncertainty around the near-term outlook.

Secondly, medical costs have been surging across Medicaid and ACA populations. Medical loss ratios [MLR], or health benefit ratios [HBR], have been rising due to increased use of behavioral health services, home care, and high-cost drugs, especially in markets such as Florida and New York. Both are areas where Centene has a significant presence. This causes the market to shrink, making it harder for the company to reach its original targets set for 2025.

Next, the management withdrew its 2025 earnings guidance. This was due to market volatility, high morbidity in new enrollees, and regulatory changes, which in turn made claims patterns look unpredictable. Therefore, Centene decided it couldn’t provide reliable forecasts, a decision that was not well received by investors. When it comes to health insurance, clarity and consistency are key for investors.

Finally, I believe that the above headwinds have been further heightened by events in the broader market; a sector rotation out of managed care stocks has put pressure on government-focused insurers, like Centene, sending share prices plunging. However, all these headwinds appear to be more temporary than structural.

Structural strengths

What’s interesting is that Centene's adjusted diluted EPS for the full-year 2025 is still forecast to remain positive, with consensus estimates in the $1.21–$1.76 range, and is a sign that the company is expected to remain profitable. Being able to stand firm in wobbly times is a testament to a strong business model, in my opinion, and therefore shouldn’t be overlooked.

Another positive factor is the balance sheet. This shows a recurring premium revenue and a manageable debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 0.64. Cash flow from operations reached $1.8 billion in Q2 2025, which means the company is able to generate liquidity and financial stability. In short, the company has predictable cash inflows even during periods of high medical costs.

Centene’s CEO and management team are now fully focusing on profitability over growth. The company is also recalibrating risk, and repricing the 2026 ACA and Medicaid contracts, which should go a long way to restore its earnings power and predictability.

The company is also taking big strides to get its business in shape by restructuring business lines and geographies wherever the margins are unsustainable. By actively working with state partners, the company is also targeting a composite 5% upwards adjustment in 2025 for Medicaid rates. These negotiations are critical as Medicaid represents a large revenue share for the company, and any rate improvements will offset the rising medical costs.

Another important point is that Centene is in the process of implementing digital transformation initiatives to counteract fraud and streamline eligibility verification, and in doing so, laying some pretty strong foundations for sustained operational leverage.

A number of positive industry developments support Centene as a decent investment opportunity. Firstly, Centene is not the only company to be facing an uphill struggle. Warren Buffett recently started investing in UnitedHealth and experienced many of the same problems as Centene, confirming them as temporary rather than permanent. His move also proves that managed care remains a fundamentally attractive industry to invest in.

Another sign of the temporary nature of current headwinds is that yesterday Centene’s management reiterated their previous EPS guidance, meaning that the worst in terms of downgrades is behind us. Finally, the long-term secular picture looks positive; the U.S. population is rapidly aging, providing a large target market in the future.

As a result of guidance reiteration, CNC gained almost 9% intraday on September 11. As the technical setup suggests, the share price is currently notably higher than its 10D and 50D simple moving averages [SMA], and the gap with a 100D SMA is rapidly narrowing. In my opinion, such a pattern might signal the early stages of a trend change, where buyers are stepping in and pushing the price higher after a period of weakness.

Valuation

Let’s look at current prices: Centene is trading at a remarkable discount compared to its peers. Its GAAP P/E multiple is below 8, while its P/B ratio is around 0.6x, numbers that indicate that the market is valuing the company as though it were in structural decline, which, as we can see, isn’t the case.

Centene's TTM price/FCF ratio is below 9, while the historical average is around 18, meaning there is a 50% undervaluation gap. Forward P/E ratios are also extremely low, taking into account that a relatively rapid recovery of CNC's EPS is highly likely.

The historical average [5Y] TTM adjusted P/E ratio of CNC is 12.07. An average estimate of 12 Wall Street analysts for the FY2027 EPS is $4.71. The sample of analysts looks representative. With that being said, multiplying this EPS estimate by the historical average P/E is a reliable approach to calculate the target price for the end of FY2026. That said, $4.71 multiplied by 12.07 equals approximately $57. This value is 67% higher compared to the last close of around $34. The upside potential looks compelling, no doubt.

Risks to consider

I believe investors would need to take the following risks into account:

Firstly, medical cost inflation is unpredictable, especially from post-pandemic shifts in Medicaid and ACA, and is therefore a sector risk worth considering. Secondly, Centene has to deliver on its plans to restore margins by repricing contracts and securing rate adjustments. Failure to do this would undermine the investment case.

I should also mention policy risk. Centene relies heavily on state and federal programs, and if reimbursement rate requests are denied, or regulatory changes end up reducing funding, it will impact profits.

Bottom line

A 54% drop in share price this year doesn’t seem so scary after all, a sign of turbulence rather than permanent damage. Yes, the company is currently facing challenges, but it is still making money and has a strong balance sheet. Profits are good, revealing that the company is able to weather the storm. The management team is also intent on making the right changes, both digitally and towards restoring margins. Finally, with an aging population and an ongoing need for managed care, I see the company poised for recovery in 2026. I believe it’s a sound investment opportunity and recommend a "strong buy."

data.title: Why The Fiscal Deficit Won't Necessarily Lead To High Interest Rates
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor.

There is always something to worry about.

Since the stock market came into existence, there has been a continuous parade of wars, recessions, depressions, bank runs, crashes, pandemics, stagflations, regime changes, high interest rates, negative interest rates, and about 700 Marvel movies.

Through it all, humanity has survived and continued to advance. Publicly traded companies, including REITs, have been resilient and proven themselves capable of growth and innovation no matter what.

Another apparently unstoppable force that has been at play for centuries is the national debt.

Since the 1970s, the U.S. government has almost continuously run a fiscal deficit, interrupted briefly by a period of fiscal surplus in the early 2000s as a result of capital gains from the Dot Com bubble. And for decades, people have worried about this endless ballooning of the national debt.

Almost everyone would insist that this cannot go on forever. And yet, no one can say how long it can go on. And, perhaps more critically, no one can predict how exactly it would end if it was brought to a halt.

A prevailing view is that the U.S. will lose its global reserve currency status, foreigners won't want to buy our Treasuries anymore, interest rates will surge higher, and the American economy will suffer a severe and prolonged (perhaps indefinite) contraction.

Another point that proponents of this view would note is that the U.S. has enjoyed reserve currency status for about the average length of time that previous reserve currencies enjoyed during their own respective heydays.

This pessimistic view was lent a boost from none other than Warren Buffett, the Oracle of Omaha, during the most recent Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) annual meeting when he said:

We’re operating at a fiscal deficit now that is unsustainable over a very long period of time. We don’t know whether that means two years or 20 years, because there’s never been a country like the United States. But you know, this is something that can’t go on forever . . . and it has the aspect to it that it gets uncontrollable at a certain point.

These bearish fiscal hawks might point to the recent decline of the US dollar (against other global currencies) as evidence that this process has already begun.

We doubt that.

It is true that the USD (DXY) has fallen precipitously this year, but compared to its range from the last 30 years, it is actually still around average or somewhat elevated.

Although the recent decline in the USD has been widely discussed, it has not been widely noted that even after the decline, the USD has basically returned to its pre-COVID level.

But what about the incredible surge in the price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) in recent years? Could this be a sign that gold is gradually replacing the USD as a reserve asset?

While it is true that many central banks around the world have been accumulating more gold to hold in reserve, this is not really a sign that the dollar is losing its reserve status. Contracts and loans still have to be denominated in an easily transferable medium of exchange. Physical gold is not easily divisible or transferable. It would require some trusted (and costly) third party to carry out international settlements.

Plus, there is a natural ceiling to the level that governments and central banks can accumulate gold reserves, because the larger the reserve, the greater the nation's vulnerability to fluctuations in the price of gold.

Lastly, it should be noted that there is no meaningful movement anywhere in the world to return to a gold standard, which further eliminates gold as a real threat to the USD's reserve supremacy.

Rather, the price of gold is largely driven by sentiment, often driven to new highs when government debt-to-GDP is surging higher (as is happening today).

Those who claim the U.S. is on the verge of losing global reserve currency leadership might also point to the foreign ownership of U.S. Treasury debt as a share of the total, which has declined from over 50% during the Great Financial Crisis to about 1/3rd today.

This certainly looks concerning, but you will notice that there are ebbs and flows to foreigners' share of Treasury debt over time. For example, it spiked in the late 1970s only to fall in the early 1980s. It spiked in the late 1990s only to fall in the early 2000s.

The truth is that foreigners have continued to buy U.S. government debt. They've been buying a lot of it. Since the beginning of 2019, foreigners' collective holdings of U.S. Treasuries have increased by about $2.5 trillion.

Note that foreigners did not abandon U.S. Treasuries even during 2020 and 2021, when pandemic-era fiscal stimulus resulted in a flood of new U.S. government debt entering the market.

Since 2012, the Chinese have been shedding their Treasury holdings, but the Europeans have more than offset Chinese selling.

As a matter of fact, the Canadians also expanded their Treasury holdings by almost the same amount as the Chinese shrank theirs from 2012 through the end of 2024. But in 2025, Canadians collectively dumped Treasuries to divest of U.S. assets in protest against the tariffs.

Anyway, the biggest reason why the foreigner share of Treasury ownership has fallen since the GFC is that (1) the US government has been issuing debt at a faster pace and (2) Americans have increased their share of Treasury ownership.

US households, banks, insurance companies, pension funds, mutual funds, money market funds, and state & local governments have all meaningfully increased their Treasury ownership since the GFC.

Can Americans collectively keep absorbing an expanding amount of Treasury issuance?

While the fiscal deficit was gradually expanding even before COVID-19, the pandemic blew out the deficit to a recent level of about $2 trillion per year.

If the U.S. government continues down this path of deficit spending, the country's debt-to-GDP ratio will continue to climb higher and higher:

Unlike the WWII period, which was an acute driver of government debt accumulation, the current trajectory of debt-to-GDP is secular and structural.

So, again, if foreign buying of Treasuries doesn't keep up with the pace of issuance, then can Americans absorb the balance of government debt issuance without interest rates soaring higher?

Yes, we think so.

Consider this: To finance deficit spending, the government issues debt. This involves a two-way flow of money. Someone receives the proceeds of government spending, and someone else provides the capital to fund that spending.

Money from the public is sapped to fund deficit spending, but the deficit spending also sends money out into the public.

That government spending filters through the economy and eventually into the coffers of banks, insurance companies, pension funds, affluent households, or state & local governments that then use at least some of it to buy Treasuries.

In other words, the government's deficit spending increases the savings of the private sector and state & local governments, which can then be used to finance more deficit spending.

If the economy was running out of savings with which to finance deficit spending, then we would see this show up in higher interest rates. But that is not what we observe when comparing deficits to interest rates.

In fact, there is little to no correlation between the general level of deficit spending and changes in interest rates over time.

During the early 1980s, interest rates were extraordinarily high at the same time as debt-to-GDP hovered around the low single-digits.

Around 2000, during the peak of the Dot-Com bubble when the U.S. government actually began to run a fiscal surplus, interest rates were actually rising.

Over multi-year periods of time, there simply is no meaningful correlation between the level of deficit spending and the level of interest rates.

Instead, interest rates are overwhelmingly determined by two factors: The Federal Reserve and nominal GDP.

The Fed holds heavy sway over short-term interest rates through their control of the overnight/inter-bank rate, whereas long-term interest rates basically track with nominal GDP growth -- real GDP growth plus inflation.

When the market thinks that real GDP growth and/or inflation are going higher, long-term interest rates rise.

When the market thinks that real GDP growth and/or inflation are going lower, long-term interest rates fall.

While the current situation plays a major role here, it is often the market's expectations for the near future that play an even larger role.

For example, nominal GDP growth topped 10% in 2021, but the 10-year Treasury rate never rose past 5%, because the market knew that this incredible 10% nominal GDP growth rate would only be temporary.

As we write this, the 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) is right around 4%, while the Fed's projections for 2025 put real GDP growth at 1.4% and inflation at 3.0%. Combine those two and you get a nominal GDP growth rate of... 4.4%.

For 2026, the Fed projects nominal GDP growth of 4.0%, and in 2027, the Fed forecasts 3.9% growth.

Therefore, even as the U.S. government continues to run a significant fiscal deficit, long-term interest rates should gradually come down over the next few years (assuming the Fed's economic projections are more or less accurate).

The Real Crowd-Out Effect

The traditional "crowd-out" theory of government deficit spending posits that federal government borrowing competes with private sector and state/local borrowing for a limited pool of capital, and thus, when the federal government increases its deficit spending, it incrementally crowds out other borrowers, pushing up interest rates.

But there are two reasons we aren't very worried about the traditional crowd-out effect.

First, as we argued above, deficit spending ultimately increases the pool of capital from which government debt issuance can be financed.

Second, U.S. private sector debt to GDP has declined since the GFC as households and businesses have collectively deleveraged since that painful recessionary period.

For example, here's U.S. household debt to GDP:

Household debt to GDP, which includes mortgages but also various forms of consumer debt, has fallen from 100% during the GFC to the low 70% area today.

Here's a chart from 2005 to the present, illustrating each nonfinancial sector's share of total debt in the U.S.:

As you can see, since 2005, household and state/local debt have both shrunk as a share of the total, while business debt accounts for about the same ~30% of the total.

In 2008, the business sector's debt-to-GDP ratio stood at 72.5%. In 2025, it was 72.6%. Household debt-to-GDP was 98% in 2008 and 67.6% in 2025. State & local debt-to-GDP was 20.2% in 2008 and 11.4% in 2025.

But federal government debt has dramatically increased its share of total debt.

In 2008, federal government debt-to-GDP stood at 42.5%, and by 2025 it had climbed to 106%.

This isn't a sign of the crowd-out effect, because interest rates have not been continuously rising since the GFC. Rather, it is merely an illustration of the way in which the federal government has levered up at the same time as the private sector has deleveraged.

But while we are not terribly concerned about the traditional crowd-out effect, that does not mean there are no negative consequences to sustained deficit spending.

According to the consensus of economic research, there is a crowd-out effect of sustained deficit spending, just not the traditional one.

In fact, there are really two forms of crowding out that come from sustained and/or increasing deficit spending:

  1. There is a crowd-out effect within the government's own budget. As interest expense and other forms of spending with low fiscal multipliers increase, the share of the budget allocated to discretionary spending on economy-growing investments are crowded out and shrink.
  2. As the government spends more, it commands more of the nation's limited physical and human capital, pulling resources from the more productive part of the economy to the less productive part of the economy.

While financial capital is effectively unlimited, physical and human capital most certainly are limited.

As the government spends more and the structural fiscal deficit grows, more of a society's scarce resources such as physical production capacity and human workers get allocated to certain tasks according to the government's priorities. But those activities, on average, are less productive and economy-growing than they would be if allocated by the private sector.

(We are not saying that all government jobs are unproductive or unnecessary. We're saying that the average U.S. government job is less productive than the average private sector job, and the average dollar of government-allocated capital is less productive than the average dollar of market-allocated capital.)

It isn't that money becomes more scarce and expensive for private sector investment as a result of deficit spending. It's that real resources like people and infrastructure do.

It isn't that deficit spending saps financial capital from private investment. It's that fewer people and real resources are being directed toward private investment because more of them are being directed toward government activities.

The cumulative effect of this unproductive (or at least sub-optimal) allocation of a country's resources is a rising national debt-to-GDP ratio and declining real GDP growth.

That is a major reason (along with falling population growth) why the Congressional Budget Office forecasts slower and slower real GDP growth over the next 30 years:

As you can see, the CBO projects falling population growth to have a larger negative impact on real GDP growth than the lower labor force productivity resulting from sustained fiscal deficits. All else being equal, we would assume that productivity growth would fall even faster. But AI and automation could offset a lot of negative effects of fiscal deficits.

Holding inflation constant, declining real GDP growth (partially as a result of deficit spending and the mounting national debt) should translate into lower long-term interest rates.

This relationship isn't new. It has been evident for decades now.

As nominal GDP growth has declined, so too have long-term interest rates.

As nominal GDP growth continues to decline, so too should long-term interest rates.

That is why even a sustained fiscal deficit will not necessarily lead to higher interest rates.

And, as we have been saying for a while now, dividend stocks should be major winners from a further decline in long-term interest rates. For that reason (among others), we believe our portfolios at High Yield Investor are well-positioned to outperform going forward.

data.title: How Large Can Palantir Grow
data.description:
AI was a game-changer for many firms.

That's why last week, I've written an article, "How Large Can Nvidia (NVDA) Grow - It Won't 10x Anymore". Today I aim to look at Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) through a similar scope. Answering the burning question.

Palantir catch attention of many. Including me. I've criticized it for its secretive practices, positioned as government tech defense contractor. The firm proved me wrong.

Palantir completely changed the game and its revenue distribution. 55% revenue is still generated via government, but it's commercial segment is growing at unprecedented speed, and soon will take over.

The firm's shares are up 20x in last three years. It's now 23rd most valuable firm in US with a market cap approaching $400B.

Palantir's Business

Palantir's business is split into two main segments:

  • Government: best-known for its tech-related work with US military and intel agencies (NSA, CIA, FBI). A great and sticky business, but it wouldn't scale.
  • Commercial: Palantir's future - focusing on enterprise-grade data solutions, allowing firms to combine different data sources, visualize them, and ultimately allow higher-quality decision making.

But it's 55% government-focused and 45% commercial-focused scope is changing. Palantir AI platform is starting to become a standalone product, which will define PLTR's future. AIP-as its often referred to-is scalable, easy to configure platform aimed at the integration of customer's data, in combination with LLMs, while meeting security standards. The best part? Deployment of configured AIP to client won't take more than days.

Adoption of new tools is often a challenge. And, firms generally don't want to change processes that work well. To address this, Palantir launched AIP-oriented "bootcamps", where PLTR showcases to their potential clients the platform, using the clients' data. This strategy proved extremely successful, as AI is novel technology, and it isn't always easy to imagine what it can do. Visual presentation is game-changer.

That's the reason why in Q2 Palantir closed 157 deals of at least $1M+ and within that, 42 $10M+.

In Q2, Palantir posted 8th consecutive quarter of revenue growth. Combined revenue grew 48% YoY and 14% QoQ (QoQ now is similar to rate Nvidia was growing between 2023-2025). US revenue alone grew 68% YoY. The quarterly revenue for the first time reached $1B in Q2, meaning without any growth (which is hard to imagine) Palantir is now $4B annual run rate revenue firm.

If we were to dissect further, to put into context why I am suggesting Commercial segment will be the growth driver, is because this segment in US alone grew 93% YoY. Revenue generated via government grew 53%. This gives you a good idea, that in 2-4 quarters, Commercial will take over. And that's mostly US. Many opportunities are awaiting Palantir in international waters.

What's better? The firm is highly profitable. It's operating margin in Q2 was 16.5% (and, improving). And, GAAP EPS landed at $0.13/share.

Valuation

The issue? As always - valuation.

On a trailing-twelve-month basis it's priced at 112.3x price-to-sales. A P/E of 315.

You think forward valuation is better? Yes, but its 12M-forward P/E is still at 260x.

No surprise. It appears all is going well for Palantir - massive growth, profitable business, huge addressable market, AI tailwinds, lack of competition and S&P 500 (SP500) admission.

But, as you can see from the chart below, which compares market cap. to revenue growth, Palantir's valuation far outperformed it's top-line growth. You see where I am heading with this - most of the growth comes from valuation expansion rather than fundamentals.

It's time to ask the question, whether the re-rating is deserved? I would say yes, to certain degree. Because, we cannot base fundamentals only on revenue/EPS growth. There are other facts at play, such as the size of the addressable market, the lack of competition as I've already mentioned, etc...

But, we certainly shouldn't assume this will continue forever. The valuation will need to contract - it will become a headwind. To avoid stock decline, the fundamentals will need grow fast enough to offset the decline in valuation. It's hard to imagine, $1T stock trading at 100x sales.

Palantir is pure AI/Software play. It doesn't have direct peers (its product offering is novel), but we could take businesses like Adobe (ADBE), ServiceNow (NOW), Intuit (INTU) or Salesforce (CRM) as a gauge where it's valuation stand:

It doesn't need much explanation. 5x more expensive than the 2nd most expensive - ServiceNow.

We could argue PLTR's revenue run-rate (≈$4B) is smaller compared to all the firms I've selected, which all bring in at least $12B+ annually. But it isn't smaller by market cap - due to valuation. Though, the smaller revenue run-rate unlocks more opportunities.

Palantir's Path Into The Future

First, let's recap a bit:

  • Palantir's market cap currently is $400B.
  • Current annual revenue run-rate is ≈$4B.
  • From 2018 - 2025 (assuming $4B revenue delivery this year) revenue grew at annualized rate of 36.5%.
  • Price-to-Sales (P/S) is 112x.

We already know now, the revenue growth accelerated, in last quarter being 48%. I do think we can reasonable expect at least 40% annual revenue growth in the next 2-3 years.

So, the first question is, what needs to happen for Palantir to hit $1T market cap? - 2.5x growth (excl. stock dilution)

If we would retain elevated P/S at 100 and assume 40% revenue growth, Palantir's revenue run rate would be $10B/annum and market cap would reach $1T in 3.07 year. This is very bullish scenario, because as revenue run rate increases, we would expect P/S contraction.

A more realistic scenario, in my view, is P/S at 50 and growth at 40%. In this instance, Palantir's would need to have $20B/annum revenue run-rate. Underneath this assumptions a $1T market cap would be reached in 5.13 years.

You see, I am not questioning the revenue growth. I expect this shouldn't be a problem, particularly as Palantir accelerates its expansion outside of US. What I am questioning is where the valuation should sits, as this has meaningful implications on its future growth. As I said previously, this will be future headwind.

Reaching $1T is major leap. In revenue, profit margins, scale of business and successful execution. Few of tech firms, reached this milestone.

Can Palantir do it? Absolutely. Commercial expansion - in US and outside, new US military contracts, NATO's Maven Smart System, are all levers how to pull this off. I think on the execution side, Palantir proven it can deliver, well-above expectations. Where I see risk is economy. If economy starts to weakening, as we see with just 22k job additions in August, FED being behind the curve with late rate cuts, or inflation remains sticky with latest report at 2.9%, CAPEX spending could drop, negative impacting PLTR's plans. A macro backdrop which cannot be influenced.

But, what about long-term?

The real question is what is the size of addressable market. Palantir doesn't disclose this figure and estimates are all over the place. It depends on what we consider its market is. If we consider Big Data/Analytics + AI software, the market's size now is est. around $500-800B. If we account for AI growth, it could be $1.2-2T. As you see, all over the place.

It's fair to say, that even if the market isn't limitation for now, at some point it will be. Palantir won't grow it's revenue at 40% annualized rate infinitely. I am expecting:

  • 40% annualized growth until 2028
  • 30% annualized growth in 2029 - 2031
  • 20% annualized growth in 2032 - 2033

... by the end of 2033 Palantir's potential revenue could be $35B.

The market cap will be following at different P/S:

  • At P/S of 25x = $875B
  • At P/S of 30x = $1.1T
  • At P/S of 40x = $1.4T
  • At P/S of 50x = $1.75T

Ultimately, your ROR will depend on the valuation's path forward.

Investor's Takeaway

Palantir is a millionaire-maker stock. Up 20x in last 3 years alone.

I've missed it. Unfortunately, but that's investing.

I think with Palantir the questions isn't whether it will grow, but rather whether at 30,40,50% annually? And, even more important, what will be the future valuation?

The firm deserves a premium valuation, but it's the most expensive stock in my coverage. The firm needs to execute flawlessly, scale, keep increasing its margins, retain customers and enter new markets. For now, there isn't competition, but that may change.

I expect Palantir can become $875B - $1.75T market cap firm by end of 2033.

data.title: Sun Hung Kai Properties: A Buy On Earnings Turnaround And Potential Catalysts
data.description:
I'm a bull on Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited (OTCPK:SUHJY) (OTCPK:SUHJF) (0016.HK).

SUHJY's bottom line contracted for three years (FY2022-2024) in a row following my prior June 18, 2021, write-up.

The firm has finally reached an inflection point with its FY2025 (YE Jun) results. I've also identified a number of new re-rating triggers.

Net Profit Expansion Resumes

Last week, SUHJY issued its full-year financial announcement for July 1, 2024-June 30, 2025.

Its FY25 normalized earnings went up +1% to HK$21.9B, which I find encouraging.

The group's net income declined by -4%, -17%, -9% in FY22, FY23, and FY24, respectively. SUHJY had been hurt by a heavy debt burden and weak demand in the past. These are less of a concern now.

Net debt-to-equity for the company rose from 0.17 times at the end of FY2022 to 0.21x as of December 31, 2023. This was a 10-year high coinciding with FY23's bottom line drop in the teens.

SUHJY's gearing has come down to a more comfortable 15% by the end of FY25. At the analyst briefing, it stressed that "the group will continue its prudent financial management."

Its cost of debt also decreased -0.7 ppts (percentage points) to 3.7% in the recent fiscal year. During the same time frame, the 3M "Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate," or "HIBOR," a proxy for Hong Kong or HK corporates' borrowing rates, was 310bps (basis points) lower.

SUHJY saw its FY2025 net interest expense fall 30% to HK$2.5B, thanks to deleveraging efforts and a more accommodating rate environment.

Separately, its property development EBIT grew +5% in the latest full-year period.

SUHJY's results release noted the outperformance was "mostly driven by profit from the sales of residential units in Shanghai Arch Phase 3", which "received high acclaim for their stunning views of the Bund." Travel website MakeMyTrip describes "The Bund" as "a famous waterfront area in central Shanghai, offering stunning views of the city's skyline and historic architecture."

It's all about location when you are choosing your dream home. I can understand why SUHJY's "Shanghai Arch" project has been selling well.

My view is that the stock can continue to register higher net income going forward.

I'm projecting a 20% improvement in net finance costs for the next fiscal year. As mentioned above, the enterprise has the intent to maintain a strong balance sheet, and rates are on a downtrend.

SUHJY also shared at its results call it will roll out the "last phase" of "Shanghai Arch" soon, which is referred to as "villas" that are "rare" in this part of the city. I believe that the favorable momentum from "Phase 3" can be sustained with the new launch.

My forecast is an earnings growth acceleration from FY25's 1% to FY26's +5%.

I'm Watching For Multiple Catalysts

There are future developments that could push up its share price.

SUHJY's FY2025 performance could have been even better. Its HK property segment's "Development Property" or "DP" margin more than halved from 26% for FY24 to 12% in the recent fiscal year.

It participated in HSBC Holdings' (HSBC) "Non-Deal Roadshow" this week. The bank published a report (not publicly available) titled "Key Takeaways From NDR" on September 5 detailing management's disclosures. SUHJY indicated that "the (HK) housing market has displayed good signs of stabilization amid a lower local mortgage rate." It's anticipating "better selling prices in the subsequent phases" of certain local projects.

I think we might be past the worst of Hong Kong's real estate downturn, judging by local rate trends and the company's remarks. A possible rebound in this division's profitability should be well-received by investors.

Also, HK's Chief Executive is making his annual policy address on September 17. I'm expecting favorable supportive measures for the domestic property sector.

Last year's event had the "loan-to-value ratio for home mortgages relaxed," according to the media. My take is that stamp duty reduction and a lower investment threshold to qualify for local residency are potential initiatives that the HK government might introduce.

There are also opportunities for SUHJY to become more investor-friendly.

The firm committed to distributing half of its future net profit at its FY25 results meeting. The earlier policy was a slightly inferior "40%-50%" payout.

SUHJY has never done any repurchases in its history. Its five-year mean dividend-to-earnings ratio was also higher at 52%.

I won't rule out the possibility of the group doing dividend hikes or a maiden purchase of its own shares. The stock's trailing shareholder yield is a decent 3.9% with room for further upside in the future.

You Should Consider These Risks

I would be worried, if SUHJY takes on substantial new borrowings and interest rates rise again.

Assuming that the key catalysts like improved stockholder returns and policy tailwinds aren't realized, this could disappoint the investing community.

My Concluding Thoughts

I rate SUHJY as a Buy.

The market currently values it at 0.45x P/B.

The stock was trading at 0.47-0.63x book (source: S&P Capital IQ) in FY21 when its bottom-line growth was positive.

My target P/NAV for SUHJY is 0.55x (FY2021's midpoint), implying an upside of +22% (0.55/0.45).

Its earnings have turned around, and there are visible catalysts supporting share price appreciation.
